title: CW Posets After the Poincare Conjecture
overall_summary: The paper reviews Björner's characterization of finite graded posets that occur as closure posets of finite regular CW complexes: precisely those whose lower open intervals have order complexes homeomorphic to spheres. Its main new result is that the classical Danaraj-Klee sufficient condition thin plus shellable can be weakened to thin plus homotopy Cohen-Macaulay, using the Poincare Conjecture to upgrade homotopy-sphere manifolds to actual spheres. The proof inducts on rank: links in interval order complexes decompose into smaller interval pieces, thinness supplies a pseudomanifold/no-boundary condition, and homotopy Cohen-Macaulayness restricts the possible homotopy types to contractible or sphere-like. For the original matching-complex problem, the useful content is not a direct contractibility criterion, but a toolkit for replacing geometric regular-CW verification by combinatorial poset checks, and for proving sphere-link/regularity properties via thinness plus homotopy-Cohen-Macaulay methods such as discrete Morse theory or Quillen-type arguments.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Order-complex/closure-poset observation from the introduction
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be a finite regular CW complex, and let F(K) be its closure poset, whose elements are the cells of K together with a unique minimal element hat0 corresponding to the empty cell, ordered by closure containment: σ ≤ τ when σ is contained in the closure of τ. Let Δ(P) denote the order complex of a finite poset P, whose simplices are finite chains in P. Then Δ(F(K) minus {hat0}) is the first barycentric subdivision of K, hence is homeomorphic to K. Consequently, if τ is a cell of K, the order complex Δ(F(closure of τ) minus {hat0}) is homeomorphic to a ball, and the open intervals in the closure poset have order complexes homeomorphic to spheres of the dimensions dictated by the rank differences, equivalently S^{rk(τ)-rk(σ)-2} for an interval (σ, τ) in the usual rank convention rk(cell)=dim(cell)+1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The vertices of the first barycentric subdivision of a regular CW complex are the nonempty cells of K, and a simplex in the barycentric subdivision is exactly a chain of cells ordered by incidence/closure containment. This is precisely the definition of the order complex of F(K) with hat0 removed. Since barycentric subdivision does not change homeomorphism type, Δ(F(K) minus {hat0}) is homeomorphic to K. For a single cell τ in a regular CW complex, the closure of τ is a closed ball and its boundary is a sphere assembled from lower-dimensional cells. Passing to the corresponding subposets and intervals identifies the relevant order complexes with barycentric subdivisions of these balls, boundaries, or links, giving the required ball/sphere topology.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.2 (Björner)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: A finite graded poset P is called a CW-poset if: (1) P has a unique minimal element hat0; (2) P has at least one element other than hat0; and (3) for every y in P minus {hat0}, the order complex Δ((hat0, y)) of the open interval {z in P : hat0 < z < y} is homeomorphic to the sphere S^{rk(y)-2}, where rk(y) is the rank of y and hat0 has rank 0. Björner's theorem states that a finite graded poset P is the closure poset of a finite regular CW complex if and only if P is a CW-poset.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites this theorem from Björner and does not reproduce the full proof. The necessity direction is motivated by the preceding barycentric-subdivision observation: in a regular CW complex, the closure of each cell is a ball and its boundary/link data give spherical order complexes for the appropriate lower intervals. The sufficiency direction is Björner's construction/recognition theorem: the interval-sphere condition gives enough topological control over the would-be cell boundaries to build a finite regular CW complex whose closure relation is exactly P.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Möbius/Eulerian necessary condition and thinness observation
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a finite poset P and elements x < y, Philip Hall's relation gives μ_P(x, y) = ~tildeχ(Δ((x, y))), where μ_P is the Möbius function of P and ~tildeχ is reduced Euler characteristic. Therefore, if the open interval (x, y) has order complex homeomorphic to a sphere of dimension rk(y)-rk(x)-2, then μ_P(x, y) = (-1)^{rk(y)-rk(x)-2}. In particular, a necessary condition for closed intervals of a CW-poset is the Eulerian condition that μ_P(x, y) have this value for every x < y. The rank-2 part of this Eulerian condition is thinness: whenever rk(y)-rk(x)=2, the open interval (x, y) has exactly two elements. The paper also notes that, for graded posets, thinness implies that the order complex is a pseudomanifold.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The key identity is Hall's formula μ_P(x, y)=~tildeχ(Δ((x, y))). If Δ((x, y)) is a d-sphere, then its reduced Euler characteristic is (-1)^d; substituting d=rk(y)-rk(x)-2 gives the Eulerian sign condition. In rank difference 2, the order complex of (x, y) is a discrete set with m vertices, where m is the number of elements strictly between x and y; its reduced Euler characteristic is m-1. The Eulerian value is 1, hence m=2, which is exactly thinness. The pseudomanifold assertion comes from the same rank-2 local condition: codimension-one faces of the order complex correspond to gaps of rank 2 in maximal chains, and thinness gives exactly two ways to fill such a gap.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.7 (Danaraj-Klee)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P be a finite graded poset in the setting of checking the CW-poset conditions, in particular with the required unique minimal element when one wants to conclude that P is a CW-poset. Suppose P is thin, meaning every open interval (x, y) with rk(y)-rk(x)=2 has exactly two elements. Suppose also that P is shellable, where a pure simplicial complex K is shellable if its facets can be ordered F_1, ..., F_k so that for every j ≥ 2 the intersection F_j ∩ (union_{i<j} F_i) is a pure codimension-one subcomplex of F_j, and a poset is called shellable when its order complex is shellable. Then P is a CW-poset.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites this result from Danaraj and Klee as a practical sufficient condition and does not provide its proof. Its role in the paper is as the classical route to Björner's interval-sphere condition: shellability gives strong topological control over order complexes, while thinness supplies the no-boundary/pseudomanifold condition needed for the relevant shellable complexes to be spheres rather than arbitrary wedges or balls. The main theorem of the paper replaces the shellability hypothesis by the weaker homotopy Cohen-Macaulay hypothesis.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Standard fact mentioned before Theorem 2.2: pure shellable implies homotopy Cohen-Macaulay
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: A pure d-dimensional simplicial complex K is homotopy Cohen-Macaulay if, for every face σ of K including the empty face, the link lk_K(σ) is homotopy equivalent to a wedge of spheres of dimension d - dim(σ) - 1. The paper recalls the standard fact that every pure shellable simplicial complex is homotopy Cohen-Macaulay.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper states this as well known and refers to Björner's handbook article for background. The usual proof is that shellability is inherited by links, and a pure shellable complex has the homotopy type of a wedge of spheres all in top dimension, with the ball case corresponding to a zero wedge/contractible case. Applying this to every link gives the homotopy Cohen-Macaulay link condition.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.1 (Poincare Conjecture, used as an input)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If K is a manifold that is a simply connected homology sphere, then K is homeomorphic to a sphere. Equivalently, if K is a manifold that is homotopy equivalent to a sphere, then K is homeomorphic to a sphere.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper uses this theorem as an external input and gives no proof. It notes the historical proof sources: dimension 2 follows from the classification of surfaces, dimensions at least 5 from Smale, dimension 4 from Freedman, and dimension 3 from Perelman. In the paper's main argument, this theorem is invoked after showing that an interval order complex is both a manifold and homotopy equivalent to a sphere, to conclude it is actually homeomorphic to a sphere as required in the definition of CW-poset.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.2 (Hersh main result)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P be a finite graded poset with a unique minimal element hat0 and at least one element other than hat0. Suppose P is thin: for every x < y in P with rk(y)-rk(x)=2, the open interval (x, y) has exactly two elements. Suppose P is homotopy Cohen-Macaulay in the poset/order-complex sense used in the paper: the relevant order complexes and their links have the homotopy type of wedges of spheres in the prescribed top dimensions, so in particular each interval order complex Δ((x, y)) is homotopy equivalent to a wedge of spheres of dimension rk(y)-rk(x)-2. Then P is a CW-poset; that is, for every y > hat0, the order complex Δ((hat0, y)) is homeomorphic to S^{rk(y)-2}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is by induction on rank, applied to intervals. In rank 2, thinness says that the open interval between the two endpoints contains exactly two elements, so its order complex is two isolated vertices, i.e. S^0; lower-rank cases are vacuous or give S^{-1} for the empty interval. For the induction step, fix an open interval (x, y). For a vertex z of Δ((x, y)), the link of z in Δ((x, y)) decomposes as the join Δ((x, z)) * Δ((z, y)); more generally, the link of a chain factors as a join of order complexes of smaller open intervals. By the induction hypothesis these smaller interval complexes are homeomorphic to spheres, and joins of spheres are spheres. Hence the vertex links, and therefore all local links, are spheres; Δ((x, y)) is a manifold. Homotopy Cohen-Macaulayness implies that Δ((x, y)) is homotopy equivalent to a wedge of spheres in the top dimension. For intervals of rank difference greater than 2, thinness lets one assume this complex is connected. The top integral homology of a connected manifold is either Z or 0, depending on orientability/boundary behavior, so a wedge of top-dimensional spheres compatible with being a manifold is either a single sphere up to homotopy or is contractible. The contractible alternative would make Δ((x, y)) a manifold with boundary, whose boundary would be pure codimension one. This contradicts thinness, because thinness implies every codimension-one face of the order complex lies in exactly two facets, i.e. there is no boundary. Therefore Δ((x, y)) is homotopy equivalent to a sphere. Since it is already a manifold, the Poincare Conjecture implies it is homeomorphic to a sphere. Taking x=hat0 proves the defining interval-sphere condition for P to be a CW-poset.

proof_techniques.name: Replace a regular CW complex by its closure poset and barycentric subdivision
proof_techniques.description: The paper emphasizes that the topology of a finite regular CW complex K is captured by its closure poset F(K): the order complex of F(K) with the minimal empty-cell element removed is the first barycentric subdivision of K. Thus topological questions about K can often be transferred to combinatorial/topological questions about the order complex of a poset of cells. This is especially useful because order complexes admit standard poset-topology tools such as shellability, Cohen-Macaulayness, discrete Morse matchings, and Quillen-type fiber arguments.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For F_w, one can try to define a closure poset whose elements are crossing matchings and their resolutions, ordered by face incidence. If F_w is regular or can be certified as regular, contractibility of F_w is equivalent to contractibility of the order complex of this poset with the bottom element removed.

proof_techniques.name: Certify regular CW structure via CW-poset interval spheres
proof_techniques.description: Björner's theorem, as used in the paper, gives a recognition principle: a finite graded poset with a unique minimum is the closure poset of a regular CW complex exactly when every lower interval (hat0, y) has order complex homeomorphic to a sphere of the appropriate dimension. This avoids having to analyze attaching maps directly; instead one proves local sphericality of intervals in the incidence poset.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the crossing-resolution cells of F_w produce a poset whose face incidences are combinatorially clear but whose attaching maps are hard to control, proving the CW-poset interval condition could justify treating the construction as a regular CW complex and allow barycentric-subdivision/poset methods.

proof_techniques.name: Thin plus homotopy Cohen-Macaulay as a substitute for shellability
proof_techniques.description: The main technique of the paper is to replace the strong and often difficult condition of shellability by homotopy Cohen-Macaulayness, provided the poset is thin. Thinness gives local rank-2 control and a pseudomanifold/no-boundary property, while homotopy Cohen-Macaulayness says intervals and links have the homotopy type of wedges of spheres in the correct dimensions. The Poincare Conjecture then upgrades the resulting manifold homotopy spheres to actual spheres.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: In a crossing-resolution poset, thinness may correspond to the fact that a single unresolved crossing has exactly two local resolutions. If shellability of the matching poset is hard, it may be enough to prove homotopy Cohen-Macaulayness by discrete Morse theory or a fiber-lemma argument.

proof_techniques.name: Induction on rank using link decompositions into joins of smaller intervals
proof_techniques.description: In the proof of Theorem 2.2, the link of a vertex z in an interval order complex Δ((x, y)) decomposes as a join of smaller interval order complexes, namely Δ((x, z)) * Δ((z, y)); links of longer chains decompose similarly into joins over the successive subintervals. Inductively knowing that smaller intervals are spheres implies their joins are spheres, and this proves that the larger interval order complex is a manifold.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For matchings, resolving a set of crossings may split the remaining choices into independent subproblems. If intervals in the crossing-resolution poset factor similarly, their links may be expressible as joins/products of smaller matching complexes, enabling induction.

proof_techniques.name: Use thinness to rule out boundary and force sphere rather than ball/contractible manifold
proof_techniques.description: After homotopy Cohen-Macaulayness reduces an interval order complex to a wedge of top-dimensional spheres, the manifold condition restricts the top homology to either Z or 0. The Z case gives a homotopy sphere; the 0 case gives a contractible manifold. The paper rules out the contractible case by observing that a contractible manifold would have boundary, while thinness forces every codimension-one face to lie in exactly two facets, so the complex has no boundary.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a local complex arising from F_w is known to be a manifold-like pseudomanifold, the two-resolution property at codimension-one faces may prevent boundary phenomena. This can be used to prove local links are spheres, a key step in regularity and poset-topology arguments.

proof_techniques.name: Use top homology of manifolds to collapse wedge possibilities
proof_techniques.description: A homotopy Cohen-Macaulay complex is allowed to be a wedge of many top-dimensional spheres. The paper exploits the additional fact that the complex is a connected manifold: its top integral homology is either Z or 0. Therefore the wedge has either exactly one top sphere or none, reducing the possible homotopy types to homotopy sphere or contractible.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If links or intervals in the matching complex can be shown to be manifolds and homotopy Cohen-Macaulay, then one may not need to compute their full homology ranks; manifold top-homology constraints can force the desired spherical local topology.

other_useful_info: The paper explicitly says that replacing shellability by homotopy Cohen-Macaulayness broadens the usable toolkit, naming discrete Morse theory, especially Forman and Babson-Hersh, and the Quillen Fiber Lemma as methods for proving the needed homotopy Cohen-Macaulay property. It also notes that pure shellable simplicial complexes are homotopy Cohen-Macaulay, so shellability remains a sufficient route when available. Thinness is highlighted as both the rank-2 part of the Eulerian condition and as a condition implying pseudomanifold behavior for graded posets. Examples and related references mentioned include Björner-Wachs proving Bruhat order thin and shellable, hence a CW-poset, and the observation that nontrivial simplicial posets with unique minimal element are CW-posets. Anders Björner is noted as having independently obtained related Poincare-Conjecture-based results.
why_relevant: Gives criteria for posets to be face posets of regular CW complexes, especially using Eulerian and shellability hypotheses. This is useful for converting combinatorial information about crossing-resolution posets into topological conclusions.
